content:
{
  "title": "Adversarial Shadow Prompting: Self-Reflexive Prompts for Robust LLM Responses",
  "problem": "Large language models are highly susceptible to adversarial prompts and jailbreak attacks that elicit unsafe or undesired outputs. Existing prompt engineering and filtering mechanisms can be bypassed by cleverly crafted adversarial inputs.",
  "motivation": "As LLMs are deployed in safety-critical and public-facing applications, their robustness to prompt-based attacks becomes paramount. Current defense strategies are reactive, patching known jailbreaks post hoc rather than preventing new attack vectors. A new proactive prompting approach could raise the general robustness frontier.",
  "method": "We propose Adversarial Shadow Prompting (ASP), a novel method in which every user prompt is automatically prefixed with a meta-query that instructs the LLM to self-reflect on the intent, ambiguity, and policy compliance of the input. This meta-query is dynamically generated using a secondary prompt that paraphrases the original input in adversarial space—i.e., generates likely exploitative reformulations using an adversarial LLM. The LLM is then required to answer both the shadow prompt (assessing risk and intent) and the user request, and its final output is gated based on the self-reflection. This method creates an internal adversarial check before content generation.",
  "experiment_plan": "We will evaluate ASP on existing adversarial and jailbreak benchmark datasets (e.g., AdvBench, JailbreakBench, HarmfulQA). Baselines include standard prompting, prompt filtering, and chain-of-thought self-reflection. Metrics will include attack success rate, false positives, and response helpfulness. Robustness gains will be quantified by the reduction in successful adversarial generations without significant utility loss."
}
